articles.topic: naked mole rat longevity gene mice lifespan
articles.headline: Scientists Borrowed a Longevity Gene From the World's Toughest Animal. Here's What Happened.
articles.body: Naked mole rats barely age. They rarely get cancer. And they can live for decades. Scientists at the University of Rochester wanted to know if some of that biological armor could be transplanted into another species. So they did it. They transferred a gene linked to the naked mole rat's unusually high levels of a protective compound called HMW-HA into mice. The result: the modified mice stayed healthier longer, showed less age-related inflammation across multiple tissues, and lived about 4.4% longer on average. That's modest in raw numbers, but the bigger point is this: a longevity mechanism from one species was successfully moved into another for the first time.
articles.takeaway: You can't transfer genes, but you can attack the same targets: reduce chronic inflammation, protect your gut health, and stay active. These lifestyle levers appear to work on the same biological pathways the naked mole rat exploits naturally.

articles.topic: egg consumption Alzheimer's disease risk reduction
articles.headline: Eating Eggs Regularly Was Linked to a 27% Lower Alzheimer's Risk in a 15-Year Study
articles.body: A large study tracking nearly 40,000 adults over age 65 for more than 15 years found that people who ate eggs five or more times a week had a 27% lower risk of developing Alzheimer's disease compared to those who rarely or never ate them. Even modest intake helped: eating eggs just one to three times a month was linked to a 17% lower risk. The research, published in the Journal of Nutrition and conducted at Loma Linda University, used Medicare records to confirm diagnoses. Researchers point to eggs' rich content of choline, lutein, zeaxanthin, and omega-3 fatty acids as potential brain-protective nutrients.
articles.takeaway: You don't need to eat eggs every day to see benefits. Even a few times a week may matter. The choline in eggs is especially important for memory and brain cell communication, making them one of the most practical brain-health foods you can add to your routine.
articles.source: Journal of Nutrition / Loma Linda University

articles.topic: young gut bacteria liver aging cancer risk
articles.headline: Restoring Young Gut Bacteria Reversed Liver Aging in Mice and Blocked Cancer
articles.body: New research presented at Digestive Disease Week 2026 found that restoring an aging mouse's gut bacteria to a more youthful state protected the liver, reduced age-related damage, and cut cancer risk dramatically. In the study, none of the mice that received their restored younger microbiome developed liver cancer. In contrast, cancer appeared in 2 out of 8 untreated aging mice. Treated mice also showed lower inflammation and less liver injury. The lead researcher from the University of Texas Medical Branch said the aging microbiome appears to actively drive liver dysfunction, not just reflect it. Human trials are being planned.
articles.takeaway: Your gut bacteria age alongside you, and that aging microbiome may actively accelerate organ damage. Prioritizing a diverse, fiber-rich diet is the most evidence-backed way to keep your microbiome younger and healthier as you get older.

articles.topic: perimenopause cardiovascular health window of opportunity
articles.headline: Perimenopause Is a Window for Heart Health. Most Women Are Missing It.
articles.body: A new analysis of nationwide U.S. data published in the Journal of the American Heart Association found that perimenopausal women are twice as likely to have a low cardiovascular health score compared to women who still have regular menstrual cycles. Researchers say this period, which can begin as early as the mid-30s, represents a critical window where heart disease risk starts rising due to hormonal and metabolic shifts. The study used the American Heart Association's Life's Essential 8 scoring system to assess cardiovascular health across women at different reproductive stages. Authors urge earlier screening for blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar during this transition.
articles.takeaway: If you're in your late 30s or 40s and noticing hormonal changes, don't wait until menopause to check your cardiovascular numbers. Getting your blood pressure, cholesterol, and blood sugar tracked now could be one of the most important preventive steps you take.
articles.source: Journal of the American Heart Association

quote.text: It does not matter how slowly you go as long as you do not stop.
quote.author: Confucius
quote.context: Chinese philosopher whose teachings on perseverance and self-cultivation have guided people for over 2,500 years.

workout.title: Back in 30: Row, Hang, and Brace
workout.tip: If you don't have a pull-up bar, loop a resistance band over a door frame or use a sturdy table edge for inverted rows. For dead hangs, even 10 seconds counts — accumulate the time across multiple short sets.
workout.warmup: Spend 3 minutes doing arm circles, shoulder rolls, and scapular retractions (squeeze your shoulder blades together 10 times). Finish with a 30-second dead hang or passive shoulder stretch from a bar or door frame to open up the lats and shoulder girdle before loading them.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with palms facing away, arms fully extended. Let your shoulders decompress. Engage your core lightly. Build to 45 seconds over time. Excellent for grip strength and spinal decompression.

exercises.name: Scapular Pull-Ups (or Band-Assisted Pull-Ups)
exercises.notes: From a dead hang, retract and depress your shoulder blades without bending your elbows. This builds the foundation for full pull-ups. If you can do full pull-ups, do controlled reps with a 3-second descent.

exercises.name: Dumbbell or Bodyweight Row
exercises.notes: Brace one knee and hand on a bench or chair. Drive your elbow toward the ceiling, keeping it close to your torso. Squeeze at the top for one second. No dumbbell? Do inverted rows under a table.

exercises.name: Superman Hold
exercises.notes: Lie face down, arms extended overhead. Lift chest, arms, and legs off the floor simultaneously. Hold 3 seconds and lower slowly. Targets the posterior chain and lower back without any equipment.

exercises.name: Dead Bug
exercises.notes: Lie on your back, arms pointed to ceiling, knees at 90 degrees. Lower opposite arm and leg toward the floor while pressing your lower back into the ground. Go slow. This is harder than it looks.

workout.cooldown: Finish with a doorway chest stretch and a 60-second child's pose to release the lats. Take 5 slow, deep breaths in child's pose to help your nervous system down-regulate after the session.

product.name: Gymnastic Rings - Double Circle Wood
product.blurb: Chris trains on rings 2x per week. The most effective upper body tool that fits in a bag.

meal.title: Smoky Red Lentil and Roasted Pepper Bowl
ingredients: Fresh parsley or cilantro to serve
meal.instructions: 1. Roast the halved bell pepper under a broiler for 10 minutes until charred, then peel and slice. 2. Heat olive oil in a pan, saute garlic for 1 minute, then add spices and toast for 30 seconds. Add lentils, broth, and tomatoes, and simmer on medium for 15 to 18 minutes until lentils are soft. 3. Season with salt, pepper, and a squeeze of lemon. 4. Serve in bowls topped with roasted pepper strips, a dollop of Greek yogurt, and fresh herbs.
meal.tip: Red lentils cook fast and don't need soaking, making this genuinely weeknight-ready. Double the batch and refrigerate up to 4 days. The Greek yogurt adds about 6g of extra protein and works like a cooling raita.

product.blurb: Measure your food for two weeks and you will understand your body better than any diet book.
